---
title             : "Context Matters: Linguistic Markers of Affective Experience in Everyday Language"
shorttitle        : "Affective Language in Context"

author: 
  - name          : "Timo K. Koch"
    affiliation   : "1", "2"
    corresponding : yes    # Define only one corresponding author
    address       : "Torstrasse 25, 9000 St. Gallen, Switzerland"
    email         : "timo.koch@unisg.ch"
    role:         # Contributorship roles (e.g., CRediT, https://casrai.org/credit/)
      - Conceptualization
      - Investigation
      - Methodology
      - Formal Analysis
      - Visualization
      - Writing - Original Draft Preparation
      - Writing - Review & Editing
  - name          : "Johannes C. Eichstaedt"
    affiliation   : "3"
    role:
      - Methodology
      - Writing - Review & Editing
  - name          : "Florian Bemmann"
    affiliation   : "4"
    role:
      - Data Curation
      - Software
  - name          : "Ramona Schoedel"
    affiliation   : "2"
    role:
      - Data Curation
  - name          : "Markus Bühner"
    affiliation   : "2"
    role:
      - Resources
  - name          : "Clemens Stachl"
    affiliation   : "1"
    role:
      - Conceptualization
      - Writing - Review & Editing
      - Supervision

affiliation:
  - id            : "1"
    institution   : "Institute of Behavioral Science and Technology, University of St. Gallen"
  - id            : "2"
    institution   : "Department of Psychology, Ludwig-Maximilan-Universiät München"
  - id            : "3"
    institution   : "Department of Psychology, Stanford University"
  - id            : "4"
    institution   : "Media Informatics Group, Ludwig-Maximilians-Universität München"

authornote: |
  Conflict of interest: none
  Acknowledgements: We thank the Leibniz Institute for Psychology (ZPID) for their support with data collection. This project was supported by SNF grant No. 215303 and by a scholarship of the German Academic Scholarship Foundation awarded to Timo Koch.

abstract: |
  Research suggests that digital traces of written language, such as social media posts, offer a unique window into our emotional lives. However, those studies rest on the critical assumption that people’s language use, for instance of emotion words (e.g., "happy" and "sad"), is a reflection of their subjective emotion experience in a given moment. In this work, we test this assumption by investigating associations of self-reported emotion experience collected using the experience sampling method and everyday language characteristics logged with smartphones. Specifically, we investigate (in-sample) associations of self-reported affective experience with language features logged with smartphones in everyday life. We also distinguish between as well different time aggregations (trait, weekly, daily, momentary). From a data set of more than 10 million typed words from 486 participants, we extract features regarding word use based on word sentiment dictionaries. Morever, we take the context of language production into consideration. Specifically, gender, temporal context and communication context - private (e.g., messaging on WhatsApp) and public communication contexts (e.g., posting on Facebook). We identify distinct affect-linked language variations across communication contexts and time frames. Finally, we discuss implications for the assessment of subjective affect experience using occurrence-counts, such as word dictionaries, and opportunities leveraging smartphones to collect language data in the wild.
  
keywords          : "Affect, Language, Text, Smartphones, Experience Sampling"
wordcount         : "5000"

bibliography      : ["docs/affect_smartphone_language.bib"]

floatsintext      : no
figurelist        : no
tablelist         : no
footnotelist      : no
linenumbers       : no
mask              : no
draft             : no

documentclass     : "apa6"
classoption       : "man"
output            : papaja::apa6_pdf
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library("papaja")
r_refs("r-references.bib")
```

```{r analysis-preferences, echo = FALSE}
# Seed for random number generation
set.seed(42)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache.extra = knitr::rand_seed)

affect_language <- readRDS(file="/data/affect_language_features/affect_language.RData")

```

# Introduction

<!-- Catchy first intro paragraph: Words can reveal how we feel  - relevance and link to study, short summary -->
Prior research studies suggest that the words we use in textual language reveal how we feel [@vineNaturalEmotionVocabularies2020; @tovDetectingWellbeingComputerized2013]. As digital footprints in the form of text, such as on blog posts, social media posts, or instant messages, have become ubiquitous, new opportunities to investigate affect-linked language have emerged. In this manner, Facebook posts have been found to be predictive of emotions [@eichstaedtTrackingFluctuationsPsychological2020; @preotiuc-pietroModellingValenceArousal2016]. Moreover, researchers predicted affective disorders, for example depression, from digital text data, such as Facebook posts [@eichstaedtFacebookLanguagePredicts2018], tweets [@dechoudhurySocialMediaMeasurement2013], and text messages [@liuRelationshipTextMessage2021]. This research field is grounded on the critical assumption that the language one uses (i.e., the words) reflects how they feel in a given moment. However, recent works question the associations of and predictions from word use and subjective affect experience [@kelleyMachineLearningLanguage2022; @krossDoesCountingEmotion2019; @sunLanguageWellbeingTracking2020]. Rather, language use could be linked to one's *expression* of affect, which has some overlaps with one's subjective affect *experience*, but is not necessarily congruent [@krossDoesCountingEmotion2019]. Moreover, For instance, language-based prediction models often do not generalize well to other communication contexts (e.g., from text messages to social media posts) due to each channel's specific linguistic peculiarities [@liuCrossPlatformDifferenceFacebook2022a]. The present work leverages a novel language logging approach using off-the-shelf smartphones to investigate language-linked in affect experience across different time frames and communication contexts.

## Emotional Language

<!-- How affect manifests in written language (content) for stable traits and fluctuating states  + traditional descriptive studies -->
For the investigation of linguistic associations of stable *trait* affect that is considered a personality characteristic, researchers would collect text data over a period of time, for example diary entries [@tovDetectingWellbeingComputerized2013] or text messages [@massachiSochiatristSignalsAffect2020], and correlate the use of linguistic features, such as specific word categories (e.g., positive emotion words), with self-reported scores from questionnaires. Alternatively, they would analyze language traces of affective disorders, for example depression, that are also assumed to be relatively stable in a given time window [@tackmanDepressionNegativeEmotionality2018; @eichstaedtFacebookLanguagePredicts2018]. 

<!-- researching text-affect associations for states -->
On the contrary, the investigation of the association of language use and fluctuating affective *states* (e.g., short-termed emotions and longer moods) is more challenging since researchers usually cannot ask people how they are feeling in the exact moment when they produce a piece of text. Therefore, many prior studies associated language use with tragic events that were expected to elicit strong emotions, like September 11 [@backEmotionalTimelineSeptember2010; @cohnLinguisticMarkersPsychological2004] or the death of George Floyd [@eichstaedtEmotionalMentalHealth2021]. Alternatively, researchers would hire raters to assign affective labels to collected text data, such as Facebook posts [@preotiuc-pietroModellingValenceArousal2016; @eichstaedtTrackingFluctuationsPsychological2020].

<!-- Direct linguistic markers: The case of sentiment lexica -->
These kind of language studies often rely on word dictionaries that either assign a numeric sentiment score to each single word, for example using the popular Valence Aware Dictionary for Sentiment Reasoning (VADER) dictionary [@huttoVADERParsimoniousRuleBased2014], or that count the frequency of words from a set of given word categories (e.g., positive emotion words). The most widespread psycholinguistic dictionary is the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) [@pennebakerLinguisticInquiryWord2015]. LIWC has multiple emotional word categories that contain words which are assumed to be indicative of positive or negative affect: The *positive emotion* word category includes words like "happy" and "joy" while the *negative emotion* category is comprised of the sub-categories *anxiety* (e.g., "worried), *anger* (e.g., "angry"), and *sadness* (e.g., "tear"). 

<!-- possible issues with this assumption -->
The assumption that the use of words from specific emotion word categories reflects how one feels in a given moment represents the foundation of many prior research studies. Sun and colleagues provide a comprehensive overview of research findings on the associations of positive and negative emotion LIWC dictionaries with affect in past research [@sunLanguageWellbeingTracking2020]. However, recent studies question if emotion words truly reflect momentary subjective affect experience [@krossDoesCountingEmotion2019; @sunLanguageWellbeingTracking2020].


<!-- Indirect linguistic markers: pronouns etc. -->
Apart from emotion words that serve as *direct* linguistic markers (e.g., "I'm feeling *happy*") in the form of positive and negative emotion words, researchers have also found evidence for *indirect* linguistic markers of affect, such as *social processes* and the use of *function words* (e.g., first person singular). Beyond serving as a linguistic measure these indirect linguistic markers of affect can also create insights into the processes of affect experience. For example, socializing is known to improve positive affect compared to being alone [@lucasExplainingExtraversionPositive2008]. Therefore, writing about social processes (e.g., "I am meeting *friends*") might serve as an indirect marker of peoples' positive affect that is associated with spending time with others. In a similar fashion, the use of 1st person singular that could indicate a strong focus on oneself has been liked to negative affect and depression [@tackmanDepressionNegativeEmotionality2018]. 

<!-- cite some exemplary prediction studies, do not go too much into details since prediction is not the focus of the paper -->
More recently, researchers have been employing machine learning algorithms that are able to handle large and multi-dimensional data coming from digital textual footprints to predict affect. Using this approach, scientists have, for example, predicted affective states from Facebook posts [@preotiuc-pietroModellingValenceArousal2016; @eichstaedtTrackingFluctuationsPsychological2020] . In a similar fashion, studies found affective disorders to be predictable from social media text [@eichstaedtFacebookLanguagePredicts2018; @dechoudhurySocialMediaMeasurement2013], text messages [@liuRelationshipTextMessage2021].

<!-- 1) (descriptive) challenge: Ambiguity in the prediction target for labelled data (ground truth!), "problems" with the critical assumption that peoples' language use reflects affect experience -->
To make accurate inferences about one's affect experience from collected language data, particularly for fluctuating affect states, researchers face three major challenges. First, in order to predict an author’s affective state from text, one needs to assess the author’s affect in the exact moment of text production. Since scientists cannot not ask people what affect they had experienced in the moment they had written a piece of text in the past, for example a social media post, researchers usually use raters to assign emotion labels to existing text data [@krossDoesCountingEmotion2019]. As a consequence, those studies are concerned with the associations with and recognition of *affective expression* from text rather than *subjective affective experience*. This is due to the fundamental difference of affect expression and affect experience [@krossDoesCountingEmotion2019], meaning how people subjectively feels versus the cues they express, for example through their language use, and that are interpreted by others. In this manner, recent studies leveraging the experience sampling method (ESM) to assess in-situ self-reports question the association of emotional word use and subjective experienced affect. For instance, Kross and colleagues did not find any correlations of emotional word use in Facebook posts and self-reported subjective affect experience [@krossDoesCountingEmotion2019]. In the same manner, transcribed speech samples collected with the Electronically Activated Recorder (EAR) and experience-sampled self-reports of the subjective experience of happiness did not yield significant associations with emotional word use [@sunLanguageWellbeingTracking2020].

<!-- 2) (descriptive) challenge: getting (sufficient) corresponding language data (predictor side) esp for fluctuating states -->
A second challenge that scientists face when investigating affect-linked language is to collect sufficient textual data that corresponds with affect experience data. Text that had been created in experimental settings is usually rather short and lacks ecologic validity. However, sufficient text data is needed for accurate predictions of psychological characteristics [@eichstaedtClosedOpenvocabularyApproaches2021]. In studies leveraging social media posts, there is only data available when people have posted online. Consequently, there are many gaps in the data stream when people have not posted. As a result, granular affect predictions from social media text are not feasible and only possible for larger time frames, for example weeks [@eichstaedtTrackingFluctuationsPsychological2020]. A continuous tracking (e.g., on the hourly level) of affect experience, however, requires more granular data that social media text data cannot provide for most users. More granular data would also allow researchers to not only investigate between-person differences in affect (i.e., is this person sad?), but also assess within-person fluctuations (i.e., does this person feel sadder than they usually do?) that can also be of high theoretical and practical relevance [@sunLanguageWellbeingTracking2020]. 

## The role of language production context 

<!-- 3) (descriptive) Challenge: Keeping the text production context in mind  -->
Third, recent research suggests that each communication channel has its specific linguistic peculiarities that can have an influence on affect-linked language variations and the algorithmic recognition of affect from language traces [@jaidkaFacebookTwitterCrossplatform2018; @liuCrossPlatformDifferenceFacebook2022a]. Possibly, these language variations are partially caused by differences in emotional self-disclosure across communication channels [@bazarovaManagingImpressionsRelationships2013]. However, most prior research has been based on language from only one single communication channel, such as one social media platform, due to the challenges associated with gathering textual data from the same user across platforms (e.g., Twitter posts and WhatsApp messages).

<!-- How smartphones can help overcome challenges 1), 2), and 3) -->
Novel data collection methods leveraging off-the-shelf smartphones allow researchers to overcome the aforementioned challenges. First, using smartphone-based experience sampling, subjective affective states can be assessed in-situ when they are experienced using self-reports. Thereby, there is no need for data labeling. Second, since smartphones have become our main point of communication, they allow researchers to log the majority of text one produces on a given day. Third, smartphones offer a promising opportunity to passively log textual data across communication channels (public and private contexts) by directly logging what is typed on the smartphone's keyboard or by taking screenshots [@bemmannLanguageLoggerMobileKeyboard2020; @brinbergIdiosyncrasiesEverydayDigital2021]. 

<!-- Wrap-up, RQs, goal of the paper, present tense! -->
Overall, there is robist evidence that language is a marker of emotion in the community level, but findings on the individual level are mixed. The present work aims to address this gap. 
Based on recent developments in smartphone-based language logging methods, this work investigates how affect experience is revealed through language patterns in different time frames and communication contexts. Specifically, we investigate (in-sample) associations of self-reported affect with language features logged with smartphones in everyday life and if these features allow for the (out-of-sample) prediction of affect experience. Further, we analyze which language features are most strongly associated with affect experience. Thereby, we aim to advance affect recognition from language cues and inform psycholinguistic theory of affect.

# Method

## Data Set

<!-- Data collection procedure: overall + surveys -->
Data collection for this work was part of a large six-month panel study (from May until November 2020) based on the *PhoneStudy* research app at Ludwig-Maximilian-Universität München [@schoedelBasicProtocolSmartphone2020]. Data collection was approved by the responsible IRB board. 

## Affect Assessment 
The study comprised two two-week experience sampling phases (July 27, 2020, to August 9, 2020; September 21, 2020, to October 4, 2020) during which participants received two to four short questionnaires per day. Here, self-reported valence and arousal were assessed in two separate items on six-point Likert scales among other psychological properties as part of an experience sampling procedure. Further, trait affect had been assessed using the German version of the Positive and Negative Affect Schedule (PANAS) (*alpha*~PA~ = .92, *alpha*~NA~ = .89) at the beginning of the study period [@breyerDeutscheVersionPositive2016].

## Procedure
<!-- Data collection procedure: keyboard logging -->
To capture the text participants had typed on their phones, the PhoneStudy app also included a keyboard logging module that had been adapted from the *ResearchIME* research app [@bemmannLanguageLoggerMobileKeyboard2020]. Hereby, all typed words were categorized according to the German "SentiWS" sentiment dictionary [@remusSentiWSPubliclyAvailable2010] and the latest German version of the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count [@meierLIWCAufDeutsch2019] directly on the device. Here, only the respective word categories had been logged and not raw text to protect participants' privacy. Also, for each text input, the respective app, a time stamp, and the input prompt text (e.g., "What's on your mind?" on Facebook) were logged. 

```{r affect data set, echo = FALSE}

# load data
affect_df_all <- readRDS("/data/affect/affect_df_all.RData") # overview of all affect scores

```

<!-- describe time aggregation windows (momentary, daily, weekly, trait) + language matching -->
We aggregated logged text data over four different time frames that correspond to assessed self-reports: trait, weekly, daily, and momentary. Regarding trait affect, we aggregated all text produced during the study period and matched it with the assessed scores for positive and negative trait affect. For weekly affect, we aggregated valence and arousal scores over one week (Monday through Sunday) for participants who had filled out at least one experience sampling questionnaire per day for that given week and coupled them with all logged text that had been produced in that week. With regard to daily affect, we computed the median valence and arousal for those days where participants had filled out at least two experience sampling instances and paired them with all text produced on that particular day. For momentary affect, we aggregated all text typed in a three-hour time window around (90 minutes before and after) a single experience sampling instances as done in prior research [@sunLanguageWellbeingTracking2020]. Furthermore, for the same three-hour time window, we computed the fluctuation of assessed momentary affect in valence and arousal from one's (median) affect baseline (for participants with at least five experience sampling days) across all experience sampling instances. For example, if a participant had a valence baseline of "3" and reports a "6" in a particular moment, this fluctuation score of "+3" indicated that this person had been a lot more happy than usual. The baseline score for valence showed a correlation of `r round(cor(affect_df_all$pa_panas, affect_df_all$md_valence, use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),2)` with positive trait affect and `r round(cor(affect_df_all$na_panas, affect_df_all$md_valence, use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),2)` with negative trait affect.

<!-- describe how we distinguished different communication contexts -->
To distinguish affect-linked language variations across communication channels, we used the logged information on the app that had been used to produce the respective text (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, or WhatsApp) and the input prompt text (e.g., "What's on your mind?" on Facebook). Here, we manually categorized all input prompts across all logged apps to determine the context in which the respective text had been written. For example, if a participant had produced text in the Facebook app and the logged input prompt text was "What's on your mind?", the composed text would be assigned to public communication. On the contrary, if she had used the search bar (i.e., the prompt was "Search Facebook") the produced text would neither count as private nor public communication. Thereby, we were able to generally detect and differentiate between private and public communication on a granular level. We conducted the public versus private communication analyses for trait affect (i.e., all text produced during the entire study period) because a sufficient number of participants had created enough text data to be statistically analyzed.

<!-- describe word thresholds -->
In our language analyses regarding trait affect (all text and private versus public communication) and weekly affect experience, we included participants who had written more than 500 words in the respective time frame. This threshold had been also applied in similar previous work on text messages [@liuRelationshipTextMessage2021]. For daily and momentary affect, we used a threshold of 100 words as used in related prior research [@vineNaturalEmotionVocabularies2020].

<!-- describe final data set -->

```{r data set, echo = FALSE}

# load data

files <- list.files(path = "/data/user_df/",)

affect_language <- readRDS(file="/data/affect_language_features/affect_language_500.RData")
affect_language_private <- readRDS(file="/data/affect_language_features/affect_language_private_500.RData")
affect_language_public <- readRDS(file="/data/affect_language_features/affect_language_public_500.RData")

affect_language_es_week <- readRDS(file="/data/affect_language_features/affect_language_es_week_500.RData")
affect_language_es_day <- readRDS(file="/data/affect_language_features/affect_language_es_day_100.RData")
affect_language_es_threehrs <- readRDS(file="/data/affect_language_features/affect_language_es_threehrs_100.RData")

```

`r length(files)` participants produced any text in the entire study period. `r nrow(affect_language)` of those had filled out the PANAS questionnaire and wrote at least 500 words of text on their smartphone keyboards. Of those, `r nrow(affect_language_private)` met the minimum word threshold of 500 words in private communication and `r nrow(affect_language_public)` in public communication contexts. Thus, they were included in the analyses with regard to trait affect. For weekly affect, we included `r length(unique(affect_language_es_week$user_id))` participants, who had filled out one experience sampling questionnaire each day in a given week (Monday through Sunday) and wrote more than 500 words of text on their smartphones. With regard to daily affect, `r length(unique(affect_language_es_day$user_id))` participants had completed at least two experience sampling assessments and 100 words of written text for a given day. Finally, we had `r nrow(affect_language_es_threehrs)` experience sampling instances with at least 100 words of corresponding text from `r length(unique(affect_language_es_threehrs$user_id))` participants. 
Overall, participants' self-reported positive trait affect was balanced (*M* = `r round(mean(affect_language$pa_panas),2)`, *SD* = `r round(sd(affect_language$pa_panas),2)`) and negative trait affect (*M* = `r round(mean(affect_language$na_panas),2)`, *SD* = `r round(sd(affect_language$na_panas),2)`) was low. Moreover, self-reported momentary valence was positive (*M* = `r round(mean(affect_language_es_threehrs$valence),2)`, *SD* = `r round(sd(affect_language_es_threehrs$valence),2)`).
Table \@ref(tab:datasetstable) provides an overview of descriptive statistics of the final samples across communication contexts and different time windows. The distribution of affect measures is shown in Figure \@ref(fig:affectdistribution) in the online supplements. 

```{r datasetstable, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message=FALSE}

require(dplyr)
require(kableExtra)

options(knitr.kable.NA = '') # knitr settings

# load data
affect_language <- readRDS(file="data/results/affect_language_500.RData")
affect_language_private <- readRDS(file="data/results/affect_language_private_500.RData")
affect_language_public <- readRDS(file="data/results/affect_language_public_500.RData")

affect_language_es_week <- readRDS(file="data/results/affect_language_es_week_500.RData")
affect_language_es_day <- readRDS(file="data/results/affect_language_es_day_100.RData")
affect_language_es_threehrs <- readRDS(file="data/results/affect_language_es_threehrs_100.RData")

# create table
keyboard_datasets <- data.frame(
  
  time_frame = c("Trait", "Trait", "Trait", "Week", "Day", "Moment" ),
  
  comm_context = c("All", "Private", "Public", "All", "All", "All" ),
  
  n_instances = c(nrow(affect_language), 
                  nrow(affect_language_private), 
                  nrow(affect_language_public), 
                  nrow(affect_language_es_week), 
                  nrow(affect_language_es_day), 
                  nrow(affect_language_es_threehrs)),
  
  n_user = c(nrow(affect_language),
             nrow(affect_language_private), 
             nrow(affect_language_public), 
             length(unique(affect_language_es_week$user_id)),
             length(unique(affect_language_es_day$user_id)),
             length(unique(affect_language_es_threehrs$user_id))),
  
  mean_age = c(mean(affect_language$Demo_A1, na.rm = T), 
               mean(affect_language_private$Demo_A1, na.rm = T),
               mean(affect_language_public$Demo_A1, na.rm = T),
               mean(affect_language_es_week$Demo_A1, na.rm = T),
               mean(affect_language_es_day$Demo_A1, na.rm = T),
               mean(affect_language_es_threehrs$Demo_A1, na.rm = T)),
  
  share_women = c((mean(affect_language$Demo_GE1, na.rm = T) -1)*100,
                  (mean(affect_language_private$Demo_GE1, na.rm = T) -1)*100,
                  (mean(affect_language_public$Demo_GE1, na.rm = T) -1)*100,
                  (mean(affect_language_es_week$Demo_GE1, na.rm = T) -1)*100,
                  (mean(affect_language_es_day$Demo_GE1, na.rm = T) -1)*100,
                  (mean(affect_language_es_threehrs$Demo_GE1, na.rm = T)-1)*100), 
  
  mean_pa = c(mean(affect_language$pa_panas), 
              mean(affect_language_private$pa_panas), 
              mean(affect_language_public$pa_panas), 
              NA, NA, NA),
  
  mean_na = c(mean(affect_language$na_panas), 
              mean(affect_language_private$na_panas),
              mean(affect_language_public$na_panas), 
              NA, NA, NA),
  
  mean_valence = c(NA, NA, NA,
                   mean(affect_language_es_week$valence_week),
                   mean(affect_language_es_day$valence_day),
                   mean(affect_language_es_threehrs$valence)),
  
  mean_arousal = c(NA, NA, NA, 
                   mean(affect_language_es_week$arousal_week),
                   mean(affect_language_es_day$arousal_day),
                   mean(affect_language_es_threehrs$arousal)),
  
  mean_sessions = c(mean(affect_language$typing_sessions_count), 
                      mean(affect_language_private$typing_sessions_count),
                      mean(affect_language_public$typing_sessions_count),
                      mean(affect_language_es_week$typing_sessions_count),
                      mean(affect_language_es_day$typing_sessions_count),
                      mean(affect_language_es_threehrs$typing_sessions_count)),
  
  mean_words = c(mean(affect_language$amount_words_typed_total),
                   mean(affect_language_private$amount_words_typed_total),
                   mean(affect_language_public$amount_words_typed_total),
                   mean(affect_language_es_week$amount_words_typed_total),
                   mean(affect_language_es_day$amount_words_typed_total),
                   mean(affect_language_es_threehrs$amount_words_typed_total))
  )

keyboard_datasets[,c(3:ncol(keyboard_datasets))] <- round(keyboard_datasets[,c(3:ncol(keyboard_datasets))], 2) # round numeric columns

keyboard_datasets <- as.data.frame(t(keyboard_datasets))
base::colnames(keyboard_datasets) <- NULL
base::rownames(keyboard_datasets) <- c("Time frame", "Communication context", "*N*~instances~", "*N*~participants~", "*M*(age)", "%women", "*M*(Positive affect)", "*M*(Negative affect)", "*M*(Valence)", "*M*(Arousal)", "*M*(Typing sessions)", "*M*(Words)")

knitr::kable(keyboard_datasets,
             format="markdown",
      booktabs = TRUE,
      caption = 'Overview of data (sub) sets')

```

## Keyboard Language Analyses

<!-- intro -->
We used word dictionaries (LIWC and the German SentiWS sentiment lexicon) to categorize written words directly on the device. As mentioned in the previous section, we aggregated language features across four different time frames: trait, weekly, daily, momentary. For each time frame, we aggregated all text that had been produced during all *typing sessions* (i.e., when the smartphone keyboard was opened, and text had been produced) and computed the respective variables. For example, for daily affect experience, all text from a given participant for that day was aggregated and the daily sentiment score was computed. 

<!-- Liwc, distinguish between direct and indirect markers, add detection rate? -->
We used the latest German word dictionaries from the well-established Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) [@meierLIWCAufDeutsch2019; @pennebakerLinguisticInquiryWord2015]. LIWC dictionaries, such as *1st person singular* or *past focus*, have been theoretically derived and are clustered in a hierarchical structure: For example, the *sadness* category is a subcategory of *negative emotion*, which in turn is a subcategory of *affective processes*. Here, we computed the share of words from a given dictionary category (e.g., positive emotion words) from all written words for a given time window. Since LIWC has the same word categories across languages, we can compare word category scores for our German text data with prior studies that analyzed, for instance, English text data.

<!-- sentiment, add detection rate? -->
Moreover, to make our analyses more robust [@czarnekTwoBetterOne2022], we used the German SentiWS sentiment dictionary to obtain additional sentiment scores for logged words [@remusSentiWSPubliclyAvailable2010]. SentiWS contains 1650 negative and 1818 positive words with respective word forms and corresponding sentiment weights within the interval of [-1; 1]. Using those scores, we computed the median, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum word sentiment across typing sessions for a given time window.

<!-- report dictionary matching rates for both dictionaries!! or mention in limitations?-->

## Correlational Analysis
For our descriptive analyses, we estimated the magnitude of the associations of extracted features with affect experience (e.g., daily valence) using pairwise Pearson correlation. We correlated LIWC categories based on theory with self-reported affect. 

## Predictive Modelling

<!-- describe general ml procedure, here we include ALL dictionary categories  -->
We trained two supervised machine learning algorithms on the extracted features for the prediction of self-reported affect experience. Specifically, we compared the predictive performance of linear regularized regression models (LASSO) [@tibshiraniRegressionShrinkageSelection1996] with those of a non-linear tree-based Random Forest [@breimanRandomForests2001; @wrightRangerFastImplementation2017], and a baseline model. The baseline model would predict the respective mean value for the target variable (e.g., daily valence) of the respective training set for all cases in a test set. Additionally, we included the prediction of participants' age and gender from all collected text data as a benchmark. Models were evaluated using a ten-fold ten times repeated cross-validation scheme [@bischlResamplingMethodsMetaModel2012]. For those models, where there were multiple instances per participant (e.g., multiple experience sampling days per single participant), we blocked participants in the resampling procedure to ensure that each participant is either included in the training or test set. 

<!-- compare performance to demographic predictions -->
As a data quality check we ran predictions for participants' age (*R*^2^ = `r round(mes_age[which(mes_age$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.cv_glmnet")]$regr.rsq, 2)`, *r* = `r round(mes_age[which(mes_age$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.cv_glmnet")]$regr.srho, 2)`) and gender (prediction accuracy = `r round((mes_gender[which(mes_gender$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.classif.cv_glmnet")]$classif.acc)*100, 2)`%) from all logged text suggesting that typed smartphone language allows for inferences about user demographics.

<!-- model evaluation -->
We evaluated the predictive performance of trained models based on the coefficient of determination (*R*^2^) and Spearman's rank correlation (*r*). Before predictive modeling, we excluded features with more than 90% missing values and constant or near-constant features (i.e., with less than 5% variance). Moreover, we imputed extreme outlines (more than mean +/- 4 times *SD*) in the cross-validation procedure [@schoedelSnapshotsDailyLife2022]. To determine whether a model was predictive beyond chance (*alpha* = 0.05), we carried out variance-corrected (one-sided) t-tests comparing the *R*^2^ measures of all prediction models with those of the baseline models [@nadeauInferenceGeneralizationError2003]. We adjusted for multiple comparison (*n* = 30) via Holm correction. All data processing and statistical analyses in this work were performed with the statistical software R version 4.1.1 [@rcoreteamLanguageEnvironmentStatistical2021]. For machine learning, we used the *mlr3* framework [@langMlr3ModernObjectoriented2019]. Specifically, we used the *glmnet* [@friedmanRegularizationPathsGeneralized2010] and *ranger* [@wrightRangerFastImplementation2017] packages to fit prediction models. We pre-registered our analyses before accessing the data as a transparent account of our work [@kochAffectExperienceEveryday2022].

## Sensitivity Analyses
We ran analyses with multiple configurations regarding cut off values to test sensitivity. Those can be found in the appendix. Compare aggregation over typing sessions and treating each typing session separately. 

# Results

## Language Variations with Subjective Affective Experience

<!-- Descriptive results: overall - include time and context effects directly!, focus on valence as main outcome! -->
We found a range of affect-linked language variations across communication contexts and time frames in the collected keyboard language data.

<!-- focus on key descriptive findings here, rest goes into the supplements -->

```{r corr dictionaries, echo = FALSE}

# load required data
cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered <- round(readRDS(file="/results/insights/cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered.RData"),2)
cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered <- round(readRDS(file="/results/insights/cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered.RData"),2)
cor_language_affect_private_dictionaries_filtered <- round(readRDS(file="/results/insights/cor_language_affect_private_dictionaries_filtered.RData"),2)

cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered <- round(readRDS(file="/results/insights/cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered.RData"),2)
cor_language_affect_es_day_dictionaries_filtered <-round(readRDS(file="/results/insights/cor_language_affect_es_day_dictionaries_filtered.RData"),2)
cor_language_affect_es_threehrs_dictionaries_filtered <- round(readRDS(file="/results/insights/cor_language_affect_es_threehrs_dictionaries_filtered.RData"),2)

```

### Direct Linguistic Emotion Markers

<!-- & ALL context differences for TRAIT AFFECT, direct linguistic markers first, then indirect markers-->
Different language patterns related to trait affect emerged from the logged word dictionaries in our data set (see Figure \@ref(fig:contexttraitoverview)). Moreover, Figure \@ref(fig:contexttraitdeepdive) depicts how the use of (emotion) dictionaries differs with communication contexts for trait affect. All correlations are reported in the online repository.

<!-- discuss overall corrs for STATE AFFECT, direct markers first, then indirect markers-->
Overall, correlations coefficients for weekly, daily, and momentary affect experience with word dictionaries were smaller than for trait affect. Moreover, with the time window getting smaller, the size of correlation coefficients also decreased.

<!-- direct markers & trait-->
With regard to *direct* linguistic markers of emotion, we found that median word sentiment was positively associated with positive trait affect in all logged text (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "wordsentiment_md"]`) with respective correlation coefficients being smaller for private communication (*r* = 0.09) and even negative for public communication (*r* = -0.09). Negative emotion words were negatively correlated with positive trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Negemo"]`) and positively with negative trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Negemo"]`) across all text inputs and in the sub-contexts. The same correlational patterns held true for the anger and anxiety dictionaries that are sub-dictionaries of negative emotions words. Positive emotion words did not show any relevant correlations with trait affect in any communication context in our data.

```{r directoverview, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="Correlations of sentiment dictionaries with emotion experience across communication contexts and time frames.", out.width="100%",out.height = "95%", fig.align="center", results="asis"}

knitr::include_graphics(path = "figures/affect_dictionaries_plot_overview.png")
```

<!-- direct emotion markers ACROSS time windows-->
In the same fashion as for trait affect, the median word sentiment correlated positively with weekly valence (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "wordsentiment_md"]`) and the use of anxiety-related words correlated negatively with weekly valence (*r* = -0.11). For daily and momentary affect experience, correlation coefficients of emotion dictionaries with valence or arousal got much smaller. 

### Indirect Linguistic Emotion Markers

<!-- deep dive: focus on I / We first and then show top indirect markers only, overall and across contexts -->

<!-- show ranking for top liwc cats here  -->

<!-- indirect markers: function words -->
We found a range of distinct correlations of *indirect* linguistic markers of emotion with trait affect. First, the overall use of first person singular (e.g., "I", "me") was negatively correlated with positive trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_I"]`) and positively with negative trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_I"]`). Here, correlation coefficients were much bigger for public communication (*r*~PA~ = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_I"]`, *r*~NA~ = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_I"]`) than for private communication (*r*~PA~ = -0.03, *r*~NA~ = 0.04). On the contrary, the use of first person plural (e.g., "we") was positively correlated with positive trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_We"]`) and negatively with negative trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_We"]`). These associations were stronger for private communication (*r*~PA~ = `r cor_language_affect_private_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_We"]`, *r*~NA~ = `r cor_language_affect_private_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_We"]`) than for public communication (*r*~PA~ = 0.09, *r*~NA~ = -0.16).

<!-- indirect markers: other grammar -->
Moreover, using comparisons (e.g., "similar") was associated positively with negative trait affect overall (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Compare"]`) and particularly in public communication (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Compare"]`). Also, the use of interrogatives (e.g., "why") had a positive correlation with negative trait affect overall (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Interrog"]`), again especially in public communication.

<!-- indirect markers: social processes -->
In the same manner, two sub-categories of social processes, showed distinct correlational patterns: Male references had a positive correlation with positive trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Male"]`) and a negative one with negative trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Male"]`) in public communication. Family-related words correlated positively with negative trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_private_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Family"]`) and negatively with positive trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_private_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Family"]`) in private communication. Also, words related to the drive for affiliation (e.g., "friend") had a positive correlation with positive trait affect overall (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Affiliation"]`), particularly in private communication.

<!-- indirect markers: perceptual processes -->
Words related to perceptual processes, in particular regarding seeing and hearing, were related to trait affect with the strongest effects in public communication: Here, using words from the seeing dictionary was positively correlated with positive trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_See"]`) and negatively correlated with negative trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_See"]`) in contrast to words from the hearing dictionary that were positively correlated to negative trait (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Hear"]`) affect and negatively to positive trait affect (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_public_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Hear"]`).

<!-- indirect markers: biological processes -->
Furthermore, two sub-categories of the biological processes category correlated with trait affect: Words related to body (e.g., "head") (*r*~PA~ = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Body"]`, *r*~NA~ = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Body"]`) and health (e.g., "drug") (*r*~PA~ = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Health"]`, *r*~NA~ = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Health"]`) correlated negatively with positive trait affect and positively with negative trait affect. Also, the use of words regarding death (e.g., ) (*r*~PA~ = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Death"]`, *r*~NA~ = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Death"]`) showed the same correlation pattern. Remarkably, the health and death word categories had a positive correlation with positive trait affect in public communication.

<!-- indirect markers: informal language -->
Finally, the overall use of informal language (*r*~PA~ = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["pa_panas", "LIWC_Informal"]`, *r*~NA~ = `r cor_language_affect_dictionaries_filtered["na_panas", "LIWC_Informal"]`) and of its two sub-categories swear words and netspeak correlated positively with negative trait affect  and negatively with positive trait affect across communication channels. 

<!-- INdirect markers ACROSS time windows: function words -->
Regarding *indirect* markers of emotion, function words in particular, showed a distinct correlation pattern. Weekly valence correlated positively with the use of 2nd person plural (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["arousal_week", "LIWC_You_Plur"]`), overall use of 3rd person (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_Other"]`) and its sub-categories 3rd person singular (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_SheHe"]`) and 3rd person plural (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_They"]`). In a similar fashion, weekly arousal correlated positively with 1st person plural (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["arousal_week", "LIWC_We"]`).

<!-- indirect markers: interrogatives, social processes, perceptual processes, ingestion, dives, time-->
In line with findings regarding trait affect, the use if interrogatives had a negative correlation with weekly valence (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_Interrog"]`). The use of words related to family correlated positively with weekly arousal (*r* = 0.1) and negatively with momentary valence (*r*= -0.09) and valence fluctuation (*r* = -0.11). In the same fashion as for trait affect, we found perceptual processes (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_Percept"]`) and its sub-category seeing (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_See"]`) and hearing (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_Hear"]`) to be correlated with weekly valence. Further, hearing was correlated negatively with weekly arousal (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["arousal_week", "LIWC_Hear"]`). Moreover, the ingestion word category (e.g., "eating") had a positive correlation with weekly valence (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_Ingest"]`). Further, words related to drives (r = 0.11), particularly the drive for affiliation (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["arousal_week", "LIWC_Affiliation"]`) had a positive correlation with weekly arousal. Moreover, momentary valence correlated negatively with drives (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_threehrs_dictionaries_filtered["valence", "LIWC_Drives"]`) and its sub-dictionary achievement (e.g., "victory")
(*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_threehrs_dictionaries_filtered["valence", "LIWC_Achiev"]`). Finally, the use of words related to time correlated negatively with weekly valence (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["valence_week", "LIWC_Time"]`) and arousal (*r* = `r cor_language_affect_es_week_dictionaries_filtered["arousal_week", "LIWC_Time"]`) as well as momentary valence (*r* = -0.11).


```{r indirect, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.cap= "Selected correlations of dictionaries with affective valence for logged text across weekly, daily, and momentary time frames.", fig.scap= "Selected word dictionary correlations with state affect", out.width="100%", out.height = "100%", fig.align="center", results="asis"}

knitr::include_graphics(path = "/figures/time_dictionaries_deepdive.png")
```

## Predicting Subjective Affective Experience

```{r predictionperformance, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE}

library(mlr3)

# load required data
bmr_keyboardlanguage_age <- readRDS("/results/predictions/bmr_keyboardlanguage_age.RData")

bmr_keyboardlanguage_gender <- readRDS("/results/predictions/bmr_keyboardlanguage_gender.RData")

bmr_keyboardlanguage_trait <- readRDS("/results/predictions/bmr_keyboardlanguage_trait.RData")

bmr_keyboardlanguage_week <- readRDS("/results/predictions/bmr_keyboardlanguage_week.RData")

bmr_keyboardlanguage_day <- readRDS("/results/predictions/bmr_keyboardlanguage_day.RData")

bmr_keyboardlanguage_moment <- readRDS("/results/predictions/bmr_keyboardlanguage_moment.RData")

# measures for benchmark
mes = msrs(c("regr.srho"))

# extract performance measures

mes_age <- bmr_keyboardlanguage_age$aggregate(mes)
mes_gender <- bmr_keyboardlanguage_gender$aggregate(msrs(c("classif.acc", "classif.auc")))
mes_trait <- bmr_keyboardlanguage_trait$aggregate(mes)
mes_week <- bmr_keyboardlanguage_week$aggregate(mes)
mes_day <- bmr_keyboardlanguage_day$aggregate(mes)
mes_moment <- bmr_keyboardlanguage_moment$aggregate(mes)

```

<!-- Report on overall prediction results here & also age prediction as sanity check + short paragraph on context and time effects-->
<!-- talk about overall prediction performance -->
Overall, predicted affect experience in any communication context or time frame significantly better than chance. Figure \@ref(fig:predictionoverview) provides an overview of the performance of all learners across prediction tasks.

<!-- some words about algo performance, only report on RF here, put EN to appendix -->
On average, Random Forest models performed slightly better than the LASSO algorithm across prediction tasks, indicating non-linear predictor-outcome relationships between keyboard language characteristics and affect experience across communication contexts and time frames. Consequently, we will report on the average performance of Random Forest models across cross-validation folds in this section.

<!-- performance for trait communication contexts -->
With regard to communication contexts, even though not better than chance, predictions of trait affect were slightly better for private communication (*r*~md~ = `r round(mean(mes_trait[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_trait$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_private_pa")]$regr.srho, na.rm = T),2)`, *r*~NA~ = `r round(mean(mes_trait[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_trait$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_private_na")]$regr.srho, na.rm = T),2)`) than for public communication (*R*^2^~PA~ = `r round(mean(mes_trait[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_trait$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_public_pa")]$regr.rsq, na.rm = T),2)`, *r*~PA~ = `r round(mean(mes_trait[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_trait$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_public_pa")]$regr.srho, na.rm = T),2)`, *R*^2^~NA~ = `r round(mean(mes_trait[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_trait$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_public_na")]$regr.rsq, na.rm = T),2)`, *r*~NA~ = `r round(mean(mes_trait[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_trait$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_public_na")]$regr.srho, na.rm = T),2)`). Still, in comparison, predictions on all text data combined performed best (*r*~PA~ = `r round(mean(mes_trait[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_trait$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_pa")]$regr.srho, na.rm = T),2)`, *r*~NA~ = `r round(mean(mes_trait[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_trait$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_na")]$regr.srho, na.rm = T),2)`).

<!-- performance for state time frames -->
With regard to the time frame, predictions were more accurate for larger time windows, but still not better than chance. For instance, the prediction performance for weekly valence (*r* = `r round(mean(mes_week[which(mes_trait$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_week$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_week_valence")]$regr.srho, na.rm = T),2)`) was, on average, better than for momentary valence (*r* = `r round(mean(mes_moment[which(mes_moment$learner_id == "scale.imputemedian.regr.ranger" & mes_moment$task_id  == "keyboardlanguage_threehrs_valence")]$regr.srho, na.rm = T),2)`). 

\newpage

# Discussion

<!-- mini summary of findings, use past tense here  -->
The present study generated novel insights into affective language patterns across communication contexts and time frames by analyzing everyday textual language that had been logged using off-the-shelf smartphones. For our descriptive and predictive analyses, word (sentiment) dictionaries. Beyond the distinctive (in-sample) associations of specific language features with affective experience we found, (out-of-sample) predictions of affect experience from the extracted language cues were, however, low.

## Linguistic Markers of Affective Experience

<!-- dictionaries - direct markers: discuss descriptive findings (direct and indirect markers) and compare prior studies -->
Our findings with regard to *direct* linguistic markers of emotion, even though correlations were small, are in line with the theory that emotional word use corresponds to affect experience: We found a positive correlation of word sentiment with positive trait affect and a negative one with negative trait affect. Moreover, the use of negative emotion words showed expected negative correlations with positive trait affect and positive ones with negative trait affect across channels, while positive emotion words did not yield distinct correlations with trait affect. For smaller time frames, however, those correlations decreased in size or vanished entirely.

<!-- dictionaries - indirect markers (compare to priors studies) -->
We also discovered a range of *indirect* linguistic markers of emotion in our data. For instance, in line with prior literature, we found the use of first person singular (e.g., "I" or "me"), particularly in public communication, to be positively associated with negative affect and negatively associated with positive affect [@tackmanDepressionNegativeEmotionality2018]. On the contrary, the use of first person plural (e.g., "we") showed a negative correlation with negative trait affect and a positive correlation with positive trait affect as found in prior work [@sunLanguageWellbeingTracking2020]. Beyond these well-studied LIWC categories, additional noteworthy associations of indirect linguistic markers of emotion emerged in our data. For instance, the use of comparisons and interrogatives was associated with negative trait affect, particularly in public communication. Also, the use of hearing-related words was indicative of negative trait affect, while seeing-related words were associated with a positive trait affect, again particularly in public communication.

## Communication Context Matters

<!-- distinct correlation patterns in different contexts (e.g., I-talk, compare),  + what does this mean for research and practice? -->
In line with prior research on language differences across communication channels, our results illustrate the importance of considering the communication context in language analysis [@mehlHowTakingWord2013]. Depending on the communication context (private messages versus public posting), some language-affect associations were different in our data. For example, I-talk (first person singular) was particularly highly correlated with negative trait affect in public communication, but not in private communication. On the contrary, we-talk (first person plural) correlated higher with positive trait affect in private communication than in the public one.  

<!-- slightly better predictions for private communication than public communication (bc of self disclosure?) -->
Also, predictions, were slightly better for text produced in private contexts than public ones. This could be due to participants engaging in selective emotional self-disclosure in public communication, such as social media [@qiuPuttingTheirBest2012], making inferences of affect experience more challenging. The slightly better predictions from private communication could also come from the difference in sample sizes that were available for private and public communication with the latter being smaller. Overall, as a consequence from our findings, researchers and practitioners should always consider the communication context the text data had been produced in and keep in mind that findings and trained models might be specific to the given communication context and do not necessarily generalize well to other communication contexts. 

## Time Context Matters 

<!-- are there papers on liwc across time aggregation windows? No, we are the first! overall intro + example -->
To our knowledge, this work is the first of its kind to investigate how affect-linked language patterns change over different time frames. Depending on the time window, some affect-language associations changed in the analyzed data. For instance, median word sentiment correlated positively with positive trait affect and weekly affective valence but had a close to zero correlation with momentary valence. Consequently, researchers and practitioners should always consider the time scale (e.g., one's overall trait affect or affect experience in a specific moment) which they want to investigate affect-linked language in. As our results have shown, the chosen time focus might have a strong impact on the findings.

<!-- The smaller the time frame, the smaller the descriptive associations and the weaker the predictions and the smaller the correlations -->
<!-- some words about overall occurrence-count (e.g., dictionaries) based approaches and their limits -->
Furthermore, for smaller selected time windows, for instance momentary instead of weekly affect, descriptive associations (i.e., correlation coefficients) and models' prediction performance were also weaker in our data. These findings illustrate that assessing affect experience, particularly on smaller time scales, using the current occurrence-count approaches, such as word dictionaries, is limited. Specifically, our results indicate that occurrence-based metrics (i.e., counting negative emotion words or single emoji) work to an extent for traits, but reach their limits for smaller time windows, possibly since they do not consider language context. For instance, if participants wrote "*not* happy" LIWC would count one negation and one positive emotion word. While trait associations and predictions over longer periods are not as affected by the lack of context consideration, state affect in small time frames is often highly context dependent and, consequently, requires linguistic context. Therefore, researchers should be aware of the limits of occurrence-counts, like word dictionaries, especially for small time frames and use context-aware NLP methods such as large language models.

# Limitations and Outlook

<!-- Only word counting approach (this is a KEY discussion point!), low-ish detection rate, no advanced NLP models # what are implications for research and practice -->
The present work is limited in three ways. First and foremost, to protect participants' privacy, we did not log the raw words participants had typed into their smartphones and, instead, categorized words based on common word dictionaries directly on their devices. As a consequence, we did not have the raw text data that we could apply advanced NLP methods to, like topic models or word embeddings. These techniques usually show superior predictive performance compared to dictionary approaches since, among other reasons, they are able to consider language's context [@eichstaedtClosedOpenvocabularyApproaches2021; @kjellNaturalLanguageAnalyzed2022]. Word dictionaries, such as LIWC and sentiment dictionaries, on the contrary, do not recognize language context (e.g., "I am *not* happy") and rely on comparably simple word count occurrences. Furthermore, dictionaries can only detect those words from their internal word lists. As a consequence, even though there is a "netspeak" category in LIWC that is supposed to capture online slang words, dictionaries cannot detect unknown words, such as newly emerging words or words with typos. In our study, on average, 50% of typed words had been recognized by LIWC. This dictionary detection rate is smaller than those reported in prior work using, for example, WhatsApp messages [@kochAgeGenderLanguage2022] but similar to prior word based on logged smartphone language [@carlierSearchStateTrait2022]. To overcome the limitations of dictionary approaches, future studies could use pre-trained large language models and deploy them directly on participants' smartphones, which would allow researchers to extract context-aware language characteristics on the device without logging raw text data [@sunPreTrainedBERTModel2022; @niuRealTimeExecutionLargescale2020].  

<!-- taken from diss, needs to be adapted!! -->
Second, the data set only contained Android users, excluding those using iOS devices, because of the technical requirements of the logging software. However, past research suggests that the selection bias regarding participant demographics and personality for the German population is negligible (Götz, Stieger, & Reips, 2017; Keusch, Bähr, Haas, Kreuter, & Trappmann, 2020; Schoedel et al., 2022).
Finally, data collection was conducted during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. The impact of the pandemic itself and the with corresponding legislative measures have been shown to have had an influence on people’s day-to-day affective experience (Bäuerle et al., 2020; Rajkumar, 2020), their language use (Pisano et al., 2022; Romero, Mikiya, Nakatsuma, Fitz, & Koch, 2021), and their smartphone use in general (Jonnatan, Seaton, Rush, Li, & Hasan, 2022; Katsumata, Ichikohji, Nakano, Yamaguchi, & Ikuine, 2022).

<!-- we ONLY have smartphone language, extend to other language sources  -->
Third, even though smartphones have become our everyday companions, we only reported on participants’ everyday language produced on their smartphones in this study. However, people often use and switch between multiple devices every day [@reevesScreenomicsFrameworkCapture2021]. As a consequence, we were not able to capture participants' entirety of written language per day. Future work could expand the number of devices observed to include, for example personal computers, laptops, and tablets, using our language logging approach to capture all of participants' produced text.  

# Conclusion

<!-- summary of discussion: dictionaries suck for small time windows, smartphones are awesome to collect data! -->
This study employed a novel data collection approach to analyze affective language patterns across communication contexts and time frames in everyday smartphone language. We found distinct in-sample associations of language use, such as I-talk, with affect experience that highlight the importance of distinguishing between time frames (i.e., trait, weekly, daily, momentary) and communication contexts (i.e., private versus public communication). Overall, however, prediction performance of employed machine learning models was low. These findings emphasize the challenges of using occurrence-counts, such as word dictionaries, to infer subjective affect experience, particularly for small time windows. Finally, this work highlights the opportunities of employing smartphones to collect and analyze language data in everyday life. 

\newpage

# References

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{-0.5in}
\setlength{\leftskip}{0.5in}

<div id="refs" custom-style="Bibliography"></div>
\endgroup
